data.title: LQDH: Rate-Hedged Investment-Grade Corporate Bond ETF, Solid Yield And Performance Track-Record
data.description:
I recently wrote an article on the iShares Interest Rate Hedged Long-Term Corporate Bond ETF (IGBH), which invests in investment-grade long-term corporate bonds, and hedges its rate risk with swaps. The iShares Interest Rate Hedged Corporate Bond ETF (LQDH) is a similar ETF, but focusing on medium-term investment-grade bonds.

Both are incredibly similar ETFs, with extremely similar characteristics, fundamentals, and performance. I'm bullish on both, due to their above-average dividend yields, good performance track-records, and somewhat reduced risk and volatility. I would expect IGBH to marginally outperform, as it's a bit cheaper, with a 0.14% expense ratio versus 0.24%, and as longer-term bonds tend to yield more than medium-term investments. As the differences are tiny, I'm sticking with a buy rating for LQDH, but still marginally prefer IGBH.

LQDH - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

LQDH is an interest rate hedged corporate bond ETF.

LQDH's corporate bond exposure consists of an investment in the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), the largest investment-grade corporate bond index ETF in the market, and the industry benchmark. LQD is a well-diversified fund, with investments in over 3,000 securities from most relevant industries. Credit quality is good, albeit with relatively few investments in the highest-quality assets with the highest ratings.

LQDH uses interest rate swaps to hedge out its rate risk or exposure. In simple terms, these swaps are profitable when rates increase, and the profits cancel out bond price losses from higher rates. On the flip side, the swaps are unprofitable when rates decline, and the losses here cancel out any bond price gains from lower rates. Lots of math to make sure everything adds up in the end, but the end result should be effectively zero rate risk, and duration figures confirm this.

Due to the above, LQDH should see very low losses when rates increase. As an example, LQDH's share price was down 2.7% from 2022 to 2024, during which rates sky-rocketed. Although there were small losses, these were much smaller than those of LQD, the fund's unhedged counterpart, with higher total returns as well.

Conversely, price gains should be very small when rates decline. As an example, rates have slightly declined YTD, resulting in LQDH's price being up 0.6%, compared to 3.6% for LQD. Both price and total returns compare unfavorably to those of LQD.

Due to the vagaries of option prices and premiums, as well as ETF distribution requirements, LQDH's price and its distributions might move in unexpected ways moving forward. As an example, the fund might be forced into distributing swap profits to shareholders during a future period of rising rates. LQDH's bond portfolio would decline in price, and without any swap profits to buoy the portfolio, share prices would decline. Total returns shouldn't be impacted in any way though.

As an aside, I imagine some readers noticed the above explanation and analysis is quite similar to that of IGBH. Both are incredibly similar ETFs, so the analysis was bound to be incredibly similar too.

Risk and Volatility Analysis

LQDH focuses on high-quality bonds and hedges out its rate risk. Realized volatility should be quite low, as is indeed the case. Take special note of LQD in the graph below.

Drawdowns should be below-average too, but that doesn't seem to be the case, with LQDH suffering a massive +25% drawdown during the pandemic. Losses were comparable to those of high-yield corporate bonds, in spite of LQDH's higher credit quality. At least the recovery was swift, with only 5.5% in losses by late 2020.

LQDH's losses were entirely caused by bearish sentiment and selling pressure caused by the pandemic.

LQDH's losses were higher-than-average, because the fund hedges out its rate risk, and rates tend to plummet during recessions.

In my opinion, it was purely coincidental that the two factors above led LQDH to have similar losses and performance to high-yield corporate bonds during the pandemic. I would expect LQDH to outperform these bonds during any future downturn, although I'm not certain that this will be the case, and results would be ultimately dependent on the specifics of any downturn regardless.

LQDH's rate swaps should prove to be a negative during most future downturns, an important negative for investors to consider. LQDH remains a high-quality fund with negligible duration and below-average volatility.

Dividend and Performance Analysis

LQDH's corporate bonds generate around 4.5% in income, as per LQD's dividend yield, with the fund's swaps generating another 1.6%, for a 6.1% dividend yield. Although the swaps generate some income, don't think of these as income-producing investments, and focus on their rate hedge properties.

LQDH has significantly outperformed most bonds since 2022, due to prior Federal Reserve hikes. It significantly underperformed before 2022, as rates were slashed during the pandemic. It has outperformed since inception, because the relative gains from the hikes outweighed the relative losses from the cuts.

Moving forward, I would expect LQDH to perform similar to its benchmark, LQD. Rate swaps should not generate a significant amount of returns long-term, as swaps are simply not priced for this being the case. Significant short-term outperformance or returns is much more possible, and could result in outperformance for several years, but matching the performance of the benchmark remains the base case scenario.

Other Considerations

LQDH's investment-grade corporate bonds are impacted by, well, investment-grade corporate bond rates, with Federal Reserve rates having a more indirect effect on these. LQDH should underperform its unhedged peers when investment-grade bond rates decline, which might not coincide with periods of declining Federal Reserve rates. As an example, LQDH has outperformed LQD since 2024, even as the Fed has cut rates a few times.

Results are somewhat dependent on the specific timing, with similar returns for these ETFs since mid-2024, a few months before the cuts.

In any case, the cuts during the most recent cycle have not led to LQDH underperforming, future cuts might not either, and I don't believe that they will. Current guidance is for one cut next year, and that is simply too little to meaningfully impact bond prices or, at the very least, too little to overweight other factors (investor sentiment, credit spreads, expectations, etc.). At the same time, bonds seem reasonably well-priced right now, and I believe upcoming cuts are priced-in already. Conditions could always change, but as of right now I expect LQDH to perform in-line with its benchmark in the medium-term.

IGBH Comparison

As mentioned previously, IGBH and LQDH are incredibly similar funds, with similar characteristics, fundamentals, and performance. Comparing their returns since inception shows this quite clearly:

Disregard the price volatility during 2015, that was a temporary spike in the fund's discount.

Although the performance of these funds is incredibly similar, they do have a couple differences.

IGBH is cheaper, with a 0.14% expense ratio compared to 0.24% for LQDH. This is a small advantage for IGBH over LQDH, but an advantage nonetheless.

IGBH focuses on longer-term corporate bonds, which generate more income than medium-term securities. Specifically, IGBH gets its bond exposure through IGLB, which has a higher dividend yield than LQD.

Said income could be hedged out by the interest rate swaps used by these ETFs. I'm not confident that this is the case, but it could be. Both ETFs yield about the same, consistent with this.

In my opinion, and notwithstanding the above, the higher income of IGBH's underlying corporate bonds does benefit the fund, even if that isn't reflected in the fund's dividend yield. Differences in swap premiums, prices, and characteristics, could easily explain the above.

IGBH's longer-term corporate bonds are a bit more impacted by changes in credit conditions relative to LQDH. As an example, a 1% increase in default rates implies a 10% drop in price for a 10y bond, compared to a 1% drop in price for a 1y bond (the math is a bit more complicated, but the overall point remains). Expect higher losses for IGBH during downturns and recessions, as was the case during the pandemic.

Due bear in mind, both ETFs focus on quality, investment-grade corporate bonds, so long-term default rates and losses are quite low. Losses here should prove temporary, as was the case during the pandemic, with both ETFs posting positive returns by late 2020.

Overall, IGBH is a bit more exposed to credit conditions than LQDH. Although this is definitely a negative, and an impactful one during the pandemic, it shouldn't significantly impact the fund's long-term returns.

In my opinion, IGBH's lower expense ratio is the most important consideration here and makes it a slightly superior investment to LQDH. As both ETFs are quite similar, both remain a buy.

Conclusion

LQDH invests in investment-grade corporate bonds and uses swaps to hedge out its rate risk. LQDH's good 6.1% dividend yield, performance track-record, and somewhat reduced volatility, make the fund a buy.

data.title: Triton Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 3.61% and the Russell 2500™ Growth Index (R2500G) returned 10.73%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the industrials and information technology sectors detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in the communications services and consumer staples sectors contributed.

Outlook

While we continue to find compelling investment opportunities, we are cautious about imbalances in the small- and mid-cap market.

Investment environment

  • Stocks rose on positive corporate earnings news and hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed reduced its policy rate by 25 basis points in September, with the market expecting at least one additional rate cut by year-end.
  • Economic growth has been relatively stable despite some policy headwinds and slower-than-expected employment growth.

Portfolio review

While we have welcomed the resilience of the equity market, we have been concerned about pockets of speculative investor behavior. This has partly reflected exuberance around emerging technologies, such as artificial intelligence (AI) and quantum computing that have driven the valuations of some early-stage companies to levels we believe are untethered to fundamentals. Indeed, we saw outperformance by several companies that are not only unprofitable but have yet to launch products or report revenues. While not owning these stocks detracted from relative performance, we do not feel comfortable chasing performance by investing in companies we see as inherently risky, or where we have real concerns about the quality of the business model, balance sheet, or management team.

Among our holdings, Lantheus Holdings (LNTH) was a relative detractor. This healthcare company provides nuclear medicine diagnostic imaging products used for cancer detection. Shares declined after the company missed its second-quarter earnings target and reduced full-year guidance, reflecting competitive challenges for its core prostate cancer imaging product, Pylarify. We believe these are short-term issues related to Medicare reimbursement, which we believe will be rectified over the coming quarters. The management team has indicated a plan to resolve these issues, and we continue to like the company for its strong market positioning, attractive valuation, and track record of stable earnings growth.

Relative performance was also hindered by our investment in Shift4 Payments (FOUR), a provider of payments processing technology solutions to the hospitality and live education sectors. The company reported slower-than-expected growth in payments volumes, which led to slightly lower-than-expected earnings. In our view, the resulting sell-off in the stock was out of proportion to the size of the earnings miss, especially as this volume slowdown appears to be a transitory issue. We believe the company is attractively valued relative to its earnings growth outlook.

Long-time holding Ligand Pharmaceuticals (LGND) was a positive contributor. Ligand provides proprietary technology platforms that help other pharmaceutical companies formulate new drugs. Ligand licenses these platforms to customers, and it

receives milestones and royalties as partnered drugs progress through the development and commercialization phases. We believe this diversity, with “multiple shots on goal,” mitigates much of the risk inherent in developing a single compound. Investors have become increasingly excited about Ligand’s portfolio of approved biopharmaceutical products, which the company hopes will be a source of expanded revenue potential. This portfolio includes Ohtuvayre, a treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Investors became more optimistic about the global market opportunity for Ohtuvayre after Merck (MRK) acquired Verona Pharma (VRNA). Ligand also owns royalty rights to Filspari, a treatment for kidney disease that is being considered for an expanded treatment indication that may expand its addressable market.

Zurn Elkay Water Solutions (ZWS), another contributor, provides sustainable water control, filtration, and distribution systems. These included innovative residential sinks and commercial kitchen products that improve water quality and sustainability. The company also supplies water filling stations for schools that help reduce lead and other contaminants. Zurn Elkay has experienced growing demand for its products, and reported strong financial performance and guidance. We continue to like the company for its track record of innovation and strategic investment. The company has also executed well on its integration of the Elkay business, which has resulted in increased margins.

Manager outlook

We have been encouraged by the resilience of the economy

and the progress our companies have made on their growth trajectories. We believe lower interest rates could provide a tailwind for economic growth, although we acknowledge that inflation risks could limit the Fed’s latitude for rate cutting. Lower interest rates would also lower funding costs for smaller companies, and we would note that small-cap stocks have historically outperformed in the early stages of a Fed rate-cutting cycle.

We have seen other positive developments for the small- and mid-cap market, including a pickup in mergers and acquisitions and a resurgence in initial public offerings that have provided more opportunities for putting our active stock selection to work. At the same time, we remain concerned about other market dynamics, especially the increased disconnect between stock valuations and fundamentals in some parts of the small-cap market. While such market imbalances can persist over time, history would suggest that the eventual correction could be both unpredictable and dramatic. For this reason, we see our risk-aware approach to growth investment as more critical than ever. While we remain on the lookout for opportunities to capitalize on innovation and unique or improving business models, we remain selective in our investments. We continue to seek out well-managed, profitable growth companies with proven management teams, high quality balance sheets, and demonstrated earnings growth. We also will only invest when we believe valuations are supported by expected earnings growth over the next three to five years.

Triton Fund

Tickers A: (JGMAX) C: (JGMCX) I: (JSMGX) N: (JGMNX) R: (JGMRX) S: (JGMIX) T: (JATTX)

data.title: Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the third quarter of 2025.

Effective September 1, 2025, the fund name changed from Columbia Tax-Exempt Fund to Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund returned 3.78% for the three months ending September 30, 2025. For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com .
  • The fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index (SPBMUB), returned 3.00% for the same period.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as both curve positioning and security selection were additive to returns.

Market overview

The third quarter began with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act clearing both chambers of Congress and being signed into law on July 4 by President Trump. Throughout the quarter, tariff policy headlines continued, yet market reactions to trade policy announcements decreased relative to the previous quarter. While inflation and growth uncertainty remained, the U.S. Federal Reserve shifted its focus to the labor market, which showed signs of weakness throughout the period. The Fed viewed the downside risks to employment as meriting some degree of easing, despite inflation that remained somewhat elevated. After leaving its benchmark overnight lending rate unchanged at its August meeting, the Fed lowered the federal funds target rate in mid-September by a quarter point, to the 4.00%–4.25% range. Returns were meaningfully positive across all sectors of the U.S. bond market for the quarter. Treasury yields ended the quarter slightly lower across the yield curve, as reflected in the decline of the 10-year Treasury yield from 4.24% to 4.16% over the quarter.

Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the quarter. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 3.00%, while Treasuries were up 1.51% over the same period. Lower-quality municipals lagged higher-quality municipals as the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index (MBMSHY) returned 1.63% during the quarter, bringing its year-to-date return to 1.29%. A dominant theme from the first half of 2025 was the reshaping and ultimate steepening of the municipal yield curve. During the third quarter, the 1–30-year AAA slope flattened by 11 basis points (bps). (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) Longer-maturity municipal bonds materially outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts.

If a security is Not Rated but has a rating by Kroll and/or DBRS, the same methodology is applied to those bonds that would otherwise be Not Rated. Bonds with no third-party rating are designated as Not Rated. Investments are primarily based on internal proprietary research and ratings assigned by our fixed income investment analysts. Therefore, securities designated as Not Rated do not necessarily indicate low credit quality, and for such securities the investment adviser evaluates the credit quality. Due to rounding, percentages may not add up to 100.

On the technical front, supply continued to expand at a record-setting pace. New issuance totaled $150 billion during the quarter, 6% higher year over year. The typically strong summer-month technicals were late to materialize, although the market's seasonality has not performed according to the usual pattern this year. After a modestly negative second quarter in terms of flows, fund flows increased throughout the third quarter. Open-ended funds experienced $8.3 billion in inflows, while exchange traded funds (ETFs) saw $10.1 billion in inflows for the quarter. The short and intermediate portions of the yield curve benefited most from the positive flows.

Top sector weights (%) as of September 30, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, with the outperformance largely driven by curve positioning and security selection. Positive security selection contributions were led by housing, local general obligation (GO), special tax and continuing care retirement community (CCRC) bonds. In particular, longer, lower coupon housing structures performed well during the period's rally, recapturing some of the year-to-date underperformance longer duration holdings have experienced. The fund's overall long duration profile, with an overweight to the long-end of the curve, was also additive to returns. Slightly detracting from performance was an overweight to CCRCs.

Portfolio activity

Trading activity during the quarter was focused on capitalizing on strong bids, selling longer positions into market appetite. The effect saw a modest reduction in the fund's duration overweight position for the quarter, while keeping enough exposure to areas of the curve we believe will perform well, as we enter a more accommodative monetary policy environment. (Duration is a measure of a bond's sensitivity to changes in interest rates) Sales reduced 4% coupon healthcare holdings and credits whose spread levels had tightened to the point of providing limited further upside potential.

Outlook

We remain optimistic on our outlook for the municipal market, while also noting the fall season typically brings a more-challenging technical environment, with the lack of reinvestment from coupon and interest payments. We also expect issuance will continue at a record pace, and we remain optimistic about locking in attractive opportunities at elevated yields. Municipal/Treasury yield ratios are mixed, yet are attractive for potential crossover buyers, especially in longer maturities. While credit spreads remain compressed, we continue to prefer higher-quality over lower-quality credits, particularly as we see some areas of economic weakness and cracks beginning to emerge in the labor market. The relative steepness of the yield curve compensates investors for increasing maturity, and we maintain our conviction a longer-duration stance vs. the benchmark. Fundamentals remain on a solid footing. However, default rates are picking up slightly (albeit from very low levels), so credit research and active management will remain imperative to success.

Investment Risks

Fixed-income securities present issuer default risk. The fund invests substantially in municipal securities and will be affected by tax, legislative, regulatory, demographic or political changes, as well as changes impacting a state's financial, economic or other conditions. A relatively small number of tax-exempt issuers may necessitate the fund investing more heavily in a single issuer and, therefore, be more exposed to the risk of loss than a fund that invests more broadly. Prepayment and extension risk exists because the timing of payments on a loan, bond or other investment may accelerate when interest rates fall or decelerate when interest rates rise which may reduce investment opportunities and potential returns. Non-investment-grade (high-yield or junk) securities present greater price volatility and more risk to principal and income than higher rated securities. Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments held by the fund, negatively impacting its performance and NAV. Falling rates may result in the fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering the fund's income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities.
